articles.title: 7 Sandbox Escape Vulnerabilities Across 4 Coding Agent Vendors
articles.summary: Pillar Research has found sandbox escapes and boundary bypasses across four major coding agent vendors, including Cursor, Codex, Gemini CLI, and Antigravity, with most vulnerabilities occurring when agents write code that trusted components outside the sandbox would later run or treat as safe. These findings indicate that AI coding agents change the endpoint threat model, highlighting a need for more comprehensive sandbox designs that account for these changes. The research provides insight into four repeatable failure modes: denylist sandboxes, workspace configurations, safe command allowlists, and privileged local daemons.

articles.title: Meta Garbage Collection: Using OCaml's GC to GC Rust
articles.summary: Meta garbage collection is a crucial aspect of developing high-performance applications, particularly in systems programming. This approach is particularly relevant in systems programming where memory safety is paramount.

articles.title: Inertia-1: An Open Exploration to a Unified Motion Foundation Model
articles.summary: Inertia-1: an open exploration to a unified motion foundation model, offers a general representation that adapts across placements, devices, and tasks by studying the full lifecycle of motion models in a single controlled space. This approach eliminates the need for retraining on each new body placement or sensor type, providing robust results even at low sampling rates. The unified model can be pre-trained once on the wrist and then applied anywhere on the body, making it a versatile tool for activity recognition, gait analysis, and long-horizon disease prediction in various health insights.

articles.title: email encryption
articles.summary: Email encryption has evolved over time with advancements in technology and security measures. The core idea behind email encryption is to ensure secure communication between users by using open protocols like SMTP. This design allows for decentralized and centralized models, depending on the user's choice of host. However, email handling still faces limitations due to its age, including 8-bit clean issues and inefficient encoding techniques like MIME. As a result, developers are exploring alternative solutions to address these problems and improve security in modern email systems.

articles.title: ECC and DDR5
articles.summary: ECC (Error-Correcting Code) technology has been integrated into DDR5 memory, allowing it to correct single-bit errors and detect double-bit errors in data. This feature is particularly important for protecting 32-bit or 64-bit data, which requires R=6 or R=7 redundancy bits respectively. ECC RAM can be used on systems with 64-bit data width, but the extra bit that could be an extra parity may not be utilized. The use of RDIMMs (Registered DIMM) and UDIMMs (Unregistered DIMM) has led to confusion in terms of compatibility and pricing, with ECC RDIMMs being more expensive than their non-ECC counterparts.
